title: Battered sausage
subtitle: A pork sausage in crisp chip shop batter, deep-fried at home
excerpt: The battered sausage is the chip shop staple that sits alongside fish and chips. A pre-cooked pork sausage dipped in a simple batter and deep-fried until the coating is pale gold and crisp. Made at home, it is better than any takeaway version.
sourceNotes: British chip shop staple. Battered sausages have been sold in fish and chip shops since at least the early 20th century, particularly in the North of England and Scotland. The batter is the same used for fish, applied to a pre-cooked pork sausage.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the sausages ahead and chill. Dip in batter and fry just before serving.

content.text: The battered sausage needs to be cooked before battering. Frying raw sausage in batter risks the centre staying cold while the coating overcooks. Cook the sausages gently on the hob first, cool them, then dip them in batter and fry. The batter should be just thick enough to coat: it should fall from a spoon in a slow, steady drip. The oil temperature matters: 180°C is the target.

attrs.title: Deep frying safely
attrs.body: Use a deep, heavy-based pan and fill it no more than one-third full with oil. Keep a lid or damp cloth nearby. Never leave hot oil unattended, and never add wet food directly to the oil without patting it dry first. An instant-read thermometer is essential for accurate temperature control.

content.text: Cook the sausages and make the batter
Cook the sausages in a frying pan over a medium heat for 12 to 15 minutes until cooked through. Allow to cool completely. Pat dry with kitchen paper.

Mix the plain flour, cornflour, baking powder, and salt in a bowl. Whisk in the cold sparkling water or lager until the batter is smooth and drips slowly from the whisk. Do not overmix. Keep the batter cold.
Heat the vegetable oil in a deep, heavy-based pot to 180°C. Check with a thermometer. The oil should not be more than one-third full.
Dip each sausage into the batter, letting the excess drip off. Lower carefully into the hot oil using tongs. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until the batter is pale golden and crisp.
Drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately with chips and ketchup.

items.symptom: The batter falls off in the oil
items.cause: Sausage was wet, or the batter was too thin.
items.fix: Pat the sausages completely dry before dipping. The batter should coat thickly.

items.cause: Oil was not hot enough when the sausages went in.
items.fix: Wait until the thermometer reads 180°C before frying. Cold oil leads to greasy batter.

items.cause: Frying time too short, or temperature too low.
items.fix: The batter should be pale gold, not white. Fry for the full 3 to 4 minutes and adjust temperature if needed.

content.text: The battered sausage is a British chip shop staple. It is ordered alongside fish and chips as a cheaper alternative or an addition. It is closely associated with Northern England and Scotland. The home version lets you choose a good pork sausage, which makes a real difference to the result.
